definitions.name: AMCAF BASE
definitions.documentation: Returns the base address of the AMCAF data base.

definitions.name: AMCAF VERSION$
definitions.documentation: Returns the current AMCAF version number and a greetings list.

definitions.name: AMCAF LENGTH
definitions.documentation: Returns the length of the AMCAF data base.

definitions.name: AMCAF AGA NOTATION ON
definitions.documentation: Normally, AMCAF commands, which take colour values as parameter, use a normal 12 bit colour value in the format $RGB. Since machines like the A4000/A1200 there are 24 bit colour values in the Format $RRGGBB.

definitions.name: AMCAF AGA NOTATION OFF
definitions.documentation: Normally, AMCAF commands, which take colour values as parameter, use a normal 12 bit colour value in the format $RGB. Since machines like the A4000/A1200 there are 24 bit colour values in the Format $RRGGBB.

definitions.name: BANK PERMANENT
definitions.documentation: If you defined a bank as 'Work', but afterwards want this bank to be Permanently in memory and in the program, you could use Bank Permanent. This will make the bank stay resident in your program until it is erased.

definitions.name: BANK TEMPORARY
definitions.documentation: Changes a bank to Temporary-type, i.e it will be erased on the start of your program or when calling the Default-Command.

definitions.name: BANK TO CHIP
definitions.documentation: Bank To Chip is the reverse command to Bank To Fast. It moves a bank into Chip ram.

definitions.name: BANK TO FAST
definitions.documentation: Moves a bank into Fast ram, if any is available. Naturally, the bank will get a new starting address.

definitions.name: BANK CODE XOR.B
definitions.documentation: Bank Code Xor encrypts a bank in a similar way to Bank Code Add using an other algorithm. Each byte or word of the bank is combined by an 'logical exclusive or'.

signatures.presentation: Bank Code Xor.b code, startaddress To endaddress

definitions.name: BANK CODE ADD.B
definitions.documentation: Encodes the bank numbered 'bank' using the key code 'code'. To decode the bank, the same instruction has to be used with the negative key code.

signatures.presentation: Bank Code Add.b code, startaddress To endaddress

definitions.name: BANK CODE MIX.B
definitions.documentation: The third possibility to encode a bank, is with Bank Code Mix. So coded banks should be hard to decode.

signatures.presentation: Bank Code Mix.b code, startaddress To endaddress

definitions.name: BANK CODE ROL.B
definitions.documentation: Using these commands every bit in each byte or word is rotated by 'code' bits to the left. (Rol=RotateLeft).

signatures.presentation: Bank Code Rol.b code, startaddress To endaddress

definitions.name: BANK CODE ROR.B
definitions.documentation: Similar to Bank Code Rol, but this time the bits are shifted to the right instead of left.

signatures.presentation: Bank Code Ror.b code, startaddress To endaddress

definitions.name: BANK CODE XOR.W
definitions.documentation: Bank Code Xor encrypts a bank in a similar way to Bank Code Add using an other algorithm. Each byte or word of the bank is combined by an 'logical exclusive or'.

signatures.presentation: Bank Code Xor.w code, startaddress To endaddress

definitions.name: BANK CODE ADD.W
definitions.documentation: Encodes the bank numbered 'bank' using the key code 'code'. To decode the bank, the same instruction has to be used with the negative key code.

signatures.presentation: Bank Code Add.w code, startaddress To endaddress

definitions.name: BANK CODE MIX.W
definitions.documentation: The third possibility to encode a bank, is with Bank Code Mix. So coded banks should be hard to decode.

signatures.presentation: Bank Code Mix.w code, startaddress To endaddress

definitions.name: BANK CODE ROL.W
definitions.documentation: Using these commands every bit in each byte or word is rotated by 'code' bits to the left. (Rol=RotateLeft).

signatures.presentation: Bank Code Rol.w code, startaddress To endaddress

definitions.name: BANK CODE ROR.W
definitions.documentation: Similar to Bank Code Rol, but this time the bits are shifted to the right instead of left.

signatures.presentation: Bank Code Ror.w code, startaddress To endaddress

definitions.name: BANK STRETCH
definitions.documentation: Extents the bank numbered 'bank' to the given new length 'length'. During this process, the starting address of the bank is changed.

definitions.name: BANK COPY
definitions.documentation: Creates a identical copy of the bank with the number 'sourcebank' in the bank numbered 'targetbank'. The second version does only copy a specific part of the source bank into the target bank, creating a temporary bank of same content.

signatures.presentation: Bank Copy startaddress, endaddress To targetbank

definitions.name: BANK NAME$
definitions.documentation: The function Bank Name$ returns the name of bank number bank.

definitions.name: BANK NAME
definitions.documentation: Renames the bank with the number 'bank' to the 8 characters long name 'name$'. Most AMOS commands ignore this ID, but e.g the AMOS Tracker commands require a bank named 'Tracker '.

definitions.name: BANK CHECKSUM
definitions.documentation: This function calculates a checksum of a bank with specific contents. Using this checksum you can find out if the contents of a bank has been changed.

definitions.name: DISK STATE
definitions.documentation: Acquires the current state of a disk drive. 'flags' is a bitmap which holds two bits:

definitions.name: DISK TYPE
definitions.documentation: The Disk Type function returns the type of a directory. This value can be:

definitions.name: DOS HASH
definitions.documentation: Returns the hash value of a file. Only for advanced users who want to read directly from dos disks.

definitions.name: FILENAME$
definitions.documentation: Cuts the filename out of an mixed path and file string.

definitions.name: PATTERN MATCH
definitions.documentation: Pattern Match checks, if the string 'sourcestring$' matches the pattern 'pattern$'. Is this the case, so True (-1) will be returned otherwise False (0).

definitions.name: OPEN WORKBENCH
definitions.documentation: Tries to open the workbench again, if it has been closed previously. The Workbench screen can be closed using the AMOS command Close Workbench to get more memory.

definitions.name: X RASTER
definitions.documentation: This function returns the current X position of the raster beam in hardware coordinates. This value is not very accurate because the raster beam is very fast, sigh.

definitions.name: Y RASTER
definitions.documentation: Y Raster returns the current Y position of the raster beam. This function can be used to see how many rasterlines a specific part of a program takes.

definitions.name: RASTER WAIT
definitions.documentation: This function can be used to wait for a specific position of the raster beam. The x and y parameters contain the hardware coordinates from which the program should continue.

definitions.name: SET NTSC
definitions.documentation: This command switches to 60Hz NTSC screen mode.

definitions.name: SET PAL
definitions.documentation: Set Pal returns to the normal 50Hz PAL mode.

definitions.name: TURBO PLOT
definitions.documentation: Draws a dot at the coordinates x,y using the colour 'c'. This command is about 3-14 factors faster than the AMOS plot command, but does not care about logical combinations which could be defined with Gr Writing.

definitions.name: TURBO POINT
definitions.documentation: This functions requests the colour of the pixel at the coordinates x,y. It is much faster as the normal Point instruction.

definitions.name: RED VAL
definitions.documentation: This function acquires the red value of a colour. Together with the other Val functions, you can separate the colour into its three contents.

definitions.name: GREEN VAL
definitions.documentation: This function acquires the green value of a colour. Together with the other Val functions, you can separate the colour into its three contents.

definitions.name: BLUE VAL
definitions.documentation: This function acquires the blue value of a colour. Together with the other Val functions, you can separate the colour into its three contents.

definitions.name: PATH$
definitions.documentation: Returns the pathname part of a mixed path-filename string. Could be used as a kind of Parent$-Function too.

definitions.name: EXTPATH$
definitions.documentation: Adds a slash to a pathname, if it is a directory. Handy if you want to add a filename to a path.

definitions.name: RNC UNPACK
definitions.documentation: Decrunches a file packed with the RNC (Rob Northen Compression) algorithm into a bank.

definitions.name: RNP
definitions.documentation: Returns the address of the RNC-unpacked data; companion function to the Rnc Unpack command.

definitions.name: RESET COMPUTER
definitions.documentation: This very dangerous instruction reboots your Amiga. Obviously, this command never returns to the program.

definitions.name: RRGGBB TO RGB
definitions.documentation: The function Rrggbb To Rgb converts a 24 bit colour value into a 12 bits value, the other 12 bits will be discarded.

definitions.name: RGB TO RRGGBB
definitions.documentation: This function calculates a 12 bit colour value into a 24 bit AGA value. The missing bits are set to zeros.

definitions.name: WLOAD
definitions.documentation: This command loads the file named 'file$' completely into memory, storing it in bank number 'bank'. The bank is defined as 'Work'.

definitions.name: DLOAD
definitions.documentation: Just like Wload, this command loads a file into memory, but this time a Permanent 'Datas'-Bank will be created.

definitions.name: WSAVE
definitions.documentation: Saves the bank with the number 'bank' as file named 'file$' onto the current disk drive. This file contains no AMOS overhead, that means that only the pure binary data is saved.

definitions.name: DSAVE
definitions.documentation: Dsave is exactly the same as Wsave in every aspect.

definitions.name: FILE COPY
definitions.documentation: Copies the file with the name 'sourcefile$' to the file 'targetfile$'. This command allows you to even copy a file of 3 MB in size, even if you only got 100 KB of free memory.

definitions.name: NOP
definitions.documentation: This 'command' has no effect et al. It's only use is for speed testing routines.

definitions.name: NFN
definitions.documentation: This 'function' returns nothing useful. It's only used, like Nop, in speed testing routines.

definitions.name: PPTODISK
definitions.documentation: The Pptodisk command crunches and saves the bank numbered 'bank' into the file 'file$' using the PowerPacker algorithm. The optional 'efficiency' parameter determinates how good the bank is to be packed and must range between 0 (very fast, but less efficient) and 4 (best, but slow).

definitions.name: PPUNPACK
definitions.documentation: Decrunches a powerpacked file in 'sourcebank' into the bank number 'targetbank'. Is 'targetbank' a negative value, so it will be decrunched into Chip ram.

definitions.name: PPFROMDISK
definitions.documentation: Loads the file named 'file$' into memory bank number 'bank' and decruches it, if it has been packed using PowerPacker. Is 'bank' a negative value, the file is unpacked into Chip ram instead.

definitions.name: BINEXP
definitions.documentation: This function calculates the result of the exponential function 2^a, but is much faster than the normal AMOS expression. The parameter a must lie between 0 and 31.

definitions.name: BINLOG
definitions.documentation: Binlog is the reverse function to Binexp. It returns the logarithm to the value 'v' with basis 2.

definitions.name: EXTBASE
definitions.documentation: Returns the base address of a given extension's data area in memory.

definitions.name: IO ERROR$
definitions.documentation: Returns the string of the corresponding DOS error message. If no string for the error number exists, an empty string will be returned.

definitions.name: IO ERROR
definitions.documentation: The Io Error function gives back the number of the last DOS-error.

definitions.name: SCRN RASTPORT
definitions.documentation: Fetches the address of the graphics/rastport structure of the current AMOS screen.

definitions.name: SCRN BITMAP
definitions.documentation: Fetches the address of the graphics/bitmap structure of the current AMOS screen.

definitions.name: SCRN LAYERINFO
definitions.documentation: Fetches the address of the graphics/LayerInfo structure of the current AMOS screen.

definitions.name: SCRN LAYER
definitions.documentation: Fetches the address of the graphics/layer structure of the current AMOS screen.

definitions.name: SCRN REGION
definitions.documentation: Fetches the address of the graphics/region structure of the current AMOS screen.

definitions.name: CHANGE FONT
definitions.documentation: Opens a font directly from disk. This font will be taken for future text print outs.

definitions.name: FONT STYLE
definitions.documentation: This functions replaces the AMOS function Text Styles, because this one does not return the multicoloured font bit (Bit 6). Apart from this, Font Style is totally identical with the AMOS function.

definitions.name: FLUSH LIBS
definitions.documentation: This command closes all libraries, fonts and devices, which are currently not in use and tries to get as much memory as possible. During this process the PowerPacker and the diskfont.library will be 'flushed', too.

definitions.name: FCIRCLE
definitions.documentation: Fcircle is a command which has been missed in AMOS for a long time. It draws a filled circle with the radius 'r' and center x,y.

definitions.name: FELLIPSE
definitions.documentation: This command draws an ellipse, similar to the AMOS Ellipse command. However, Fellipse draws a filled ellipse.

definitions.name: CPU
definitions.documentation: The cpu function returns the identification number of the central processing unit currently installed your amiga. This number can currently lie between 68000 to 68060.

definitions.name: FPU
definitions.documentation: This function returns the number of an installed mathematic coprocessor or 0, if none is fitted. As before, Kickstart 1.3 only knows the 68881.

definitions.name: LAUNCH
definitions.documentation: The Launch instruction starts a new task which is saved on disk with the name 'file$'. The optional parameter 'stack' holds the size of the stack memory to be allocated (default value is 4096).

definitions.name: EXAMINE DIR
definitions.documentation: This command loads all information about the drawer 'directory$' into the FileInfoBlock. Additionally, the contents of the directory can be read out by Examine Next$.

definitions.name: EXAMINE NEXT$
definitions.documentation: Gets information about the next Object in the directory and returns its name. More information can be acquired using the object functions.

definitions.name: EXAMINE STOP
definitions.documentation: Aborts the reading process of a directory. After this command, you may not make any further calls to Examine Next$.

definitions.name: EXAMINE OBJECT
definitions.documentation: Examine Object supplies you with all available information about the Object named 'file$'. These datas can then be requested using the Object functions without any parameters.

definitions.name: OBJECT TYPE
definitions.documentation: This functions finds out if 'pathfile$' or the object whose data is currently in the info block is a directory or a file. In case of file 'type' is greater than 0 and smaller than 0 if the Object is a drawer.

definitions.name: OBJECT SIZE
definitions.documentation: Returns the size of a Object. The first version copies the size directly from the current info block, the second version fills the info block with other datas of the Object 'pathfile$'.

definitions.name: OBJECT BLOCKS
definitions.documentation: Object Blocks returns the number of blocks the current or the Object 'pathfile$' uses on a specific volume.

definitions.name: OBJECT NAME$
definitions.documentation: Object Name$ returns the name of the Object 'pathfile$'. This function is similar to the Filename$-Function, but acts quite differently as the existance of the file is checked, more information about the object is stored in the info block and the name is read out with correct case.

definitions.name: OBJECT DATE
definitions.documentation: Returns the date stamp of an Object as numeric value, which can be separated into year, month, day and weekday using the date functions.

definitions.name: OBJECT TIME
definitions.documentation: Returns the time of the last write access to an Object. This value 'time' can be converted into hours, minutes, seconds and ticks using the time functions.

definitions.name: OBJECT PROTECTION$
definitions.documentation: Converts the Protection Flags into a string in "hsparwed" format.

definitions.name: OBJECT PROTECTION
definitions.documentation: Object Protection returns the Protection flags of an Object. The Object Protection$ function converts this numeric value into a string in the format "hsparwed".

definitions.name: OBJECT COMMENT$
definitions.documentation: This function gives back the file note of an Object.

definitions.name: PROTECT OBJECT
definitions.documentation: Changes the Protection Flags of the Object 'pathfile$' to the bitmapped value 'prot'.

definitions.name: SET OBJECT COMMENT
definitions.documentation: This command sets the comment of the Object 'pathfile$' to the string 'comment$'.

definitions.name: SET SPRITE PRIORITY
definitions.documentation: Sets the prioritity of the sprites in conjunctions with the playfields to the value 'bitmap'. 'bitmap' is a bit mask in the following format:

definitions.name: CURRENT DATE
definitions.documentation: Returns the current system date. This value counts the days passed since 1st January 1978.

definitions.name: CURRENT TIME
definitions.documentation: This function contains the current time. This is NOT a value in the standard DOS-format as this one would require two longwords.

definitions.name: CD YEAR
definitions.documentation: Returns the year out from the DOS date stamp 'date'.

definitions.name: CD MONTH
definitions.documentation: Cd Month calculates the month of the argument 'date'. 'month' therefore lies between 1 and 12.

definitions.name: CD DAY
definitions.documentation: This function returns the day in the month of the date stamp parameter 'date'. The result 'day' will be a value between 1 and 31.

definitions.name: CD WEEKDAY
definitions.documentation: Returns the weekday of the parameter 'date'. 'weekday' can range between 1 (monday) and 7 (sunday).

definitions.name: CT HOUR
definitions.documentation: Separates the hour from the packed time.

definitions.name: CT MINUTE
definitions.documentation: Returns the number of minutes passed out from the longword 'time'.

definitions.name: CT SECOND
definitions.documentation: The Ct Second function returns the number of seconds from the parameter 'time'.

definitions.name: CT TICK
definitions.documentation: Calculates the number of vertical blanks (=1/50 of a second) from the parameter 'time'.

definitions.name: MASK COPY
definitions.documentation: Copies a part of a screen to an other, just like Screen Copy. However, a mask bitplane can be given.

signatures.presentation: Mask Copy screen To bank, maskaddress
Mask Copy screen1, x1, y1, x2, y2 To screen2, x3, y3, maskaddress
Mask Copy screen1, x1, y1, x2, y2 To screen2, x3, y3, maskaddress, maskbank

definitions.name: SCANSTR$
definitions.documentation: This very handy function returns the name of a key according to the parameter 'scancode', which can be fetched using the AMOS Scancode function. If there is no key for the scancode, an empty string will be returned.

definitions.name: CHR.W$
definitions.documentation: This function converts a number into a 2 bytes string. The upper 16 bits of the value are ignored and therefore you should only use values from 0 to 65535.

definitions.name: CHR.L$
definitions.documentation: The Chr.l$ function converts a number into a 4 bytes string. 'long' can be any number you like.

definitions.name: PJOY
definitions.documentation: Corresponds to the AMOS function Joy, with the difference, that one of the parallel port joysticks is checked instead of the normal joysticks. 'j' must be either 0 or 1.

definitions.name: PJLEFT
definitions.documentation: Corresponds to the Jleft(j) function for normal joysticks. Pjleft tests, if the joystick in the parallel port is currently pressed to the left.

definitions.name: PJRIGHT
definitions.documentation: Corresponds to the Jright(j) function for normal joysticks. Pjright tests, if the joystick in the parallel port is currently pressed to the right.

definitions.name: PJUP
definitions.documentation: Corresponds to the Jup(j) function for normal joysticks. Pjup tests, if the joystick in the parallel port is currently pressed up.

definitions.name: PJDOWN
definitions.documentation: Corresponds to the Jdown(j) function for normal joysticks. Pjdown tests, if the joystick in the parallel port is currently pressed down.

definitions.name: PFIRE
definitions.documentation: Corresponds to the Fire(j) function for normal joysticks. Fire(j) tests, if the firebutton for the joystick in the parallel port is currently pressed.

definitions.name: LSL
definitions.documentation: Rotates the number 'v' to the left by 'n' bits. I.e that a number 'v', which is shifted one bit to the left contains the value v*2, with two bits v*4, with 3 bits v*8 etc.

definitions.name: LSR
definitions.documentation: Shifts a number 'v' to the right by 'n' bits. This function does the same as a division by 2^n, but is much quicker.

definitions.name: WORDSWAP
definitions.documentation: Swaps the upper and the lower Word of a value.

definitions.name: AUDIO LOCK
definitions.documentation: When you start AMOS, the audio.device will be not informed, that AMOS wants to have the audio channels. Due to this flaw, other programs that are running in the background can replay a sound at any time, which will irritate the AMOS sound system.

definitions.name: AUDIO FREE
definitions.documentation: Using Audio Free you can free previously allocated sound channels again.

definitions.name: CONVERT GREY
definitions.documentation: Using this command you can convert any screen into a grey scale image, even HAM and ExtraHalfBright screens are supported. The only thing you have to do, is to open the screen and set the correct palette.

definitions.name: ASC.W
definitions.documentation: Asc.w is used to convert a word string to a number value. Therefore the result will be between 0 and 65535.

definitions.name: ASC.L
definitions.documentation: The Asc.l-function converts a 4 bytes long string back into a number. This value can range between -2147483648 and +2147483647.

definitions.name: AMOS TASK
definitions.documentation: For advanced programmers. This function returns the address of the AMOS task structure.

definitions.name: AMOS CLI
definitions.documentation: This function gives back the number of the cli process out of which the program/AMOS has been started off or zero, if AMOS has been started from Workbench. This gives you the choice to either interprete options from the command line or from the tool types of the appropriate icon.

definitions.name: COMMAND NAME$
definitions.documentation: Returns the file name of the program under which AMOS or the compiled program has been started. This is required for example to read the own Tool Types.

definitions.name: TOOL TYPES$
definitions.documentation: With this function you can acquire the Tool Type of an icon. The supplied file 'filename$' must not have a '.info' appended!

definitions.name: HAM COLOUR
definitions.documentation: This function calculates the new colour value, that is created, when plotting a pixel in colour 'c' directly behind the last point.

definitions.name: HAM BEST
definitions.documentation: As you cannot achieve the desired colour by plotting only one pixel in HAM mode, you can use the Ham Best function to search the best colour for the next dot. 'newrgb' holds the target colour and 'oldrgb' the colour of the pixel exactly before the current dot.

definitions.name: GLUE COLOUR
definitions.documentation: Combines red, green and blue value of a colour to a normal colour value.

definitions.name: PTILE BANK
definitions.documentation: Sets the bank to use for Ptiles. Actually, you should not read this command description.

definitions.name: PASTE PTILE
definitions.documentation: Places a Ptile block at the postion x,y. These coordinates must be given as block positions, that means that position 1,4 corresponds to the screen coordinates 16,64.

definitions.name: EXTDEFAULT
definitions.documentation: This command has been written especially for advanced users. With this one you can call the routine in the AMOS extension in slot 'extnb' which is normally executed after the start of a program or by the Default command.

definitions.name: EXTREMOVE
definitions.documentation: The Extremove command removes the extension in the slot 'extnb' from memory like when exiting AMOS. After this command no further instructions requiring this extension may be called.

definitions.name: EXTREINIT
definitions.documentation: This command causes a restart of the extension numbered 'extnb'. Extreinit may only be called on a extension that has been removed using Extremove.

definitions.name: TD STARS BANK
definitions.documentation: This instruction reserves a bank for 3D stars. The 'bank' parameter holds the number of the memory bank which should be used for the stars.

definitions.name: TD STARS LIMIT
definitions.documentation: Limits the stars into a specific area on the screen. If no parameters are given, the full screen sizes are used.

signatures.presentation: Td Stars Limit x1, y1 To x2, y2

definitions.name: TD STARS ORIGIN
definitions.documentation: Sets the origin, where stars start from, as soon as they have left the screen. The coordinates x,y must lie on the screen and within the drawing area, you have defined using Td Stars Limit earlier.

definitions.name: TD STARS INIT
definitions.documentation: Initialises the stars. That means, that the stars are moved by random values to avoid that they all start in the origin.

definitions.name: TD STARS SINGLE DO
definitions.documentation: Clears, draws and moves all the stars in one single step. For Double Buffered screens you have to call the command Td Stars Double Do, for normal ones Td Stars Single Do.

definitions.name: TD STARS DOUBLE DO
definitions.documentation: Clears, draws and moves all the stars in one single step. For Double Buffered screens you have to call the command Td Stars Double Do, for normal ones Td Stars Single Do.

definitions.name: TD STARS SINGLE DEL
definitions.documentation: Wipes the stars from the screen. You have to distinguish between Single and Double Buffered screens.

definitions.name: TD STARS DOUBLE DEL
definitions.documentation: Wipes the stars from the screen. You have to distinguish between Single and Double Buffered screens.

definitions.name: TD STARS MOVE
definitions.documentation: The Td Stars Move instruction moves all the stars by on step. To see the result, you have to draw the stars with a call to Td Stars Draw.

definitions.name: TD STARS DRAW
definitions.documentation: This command draws all the stars onto screen. To clear the stars again you have to use either Td Stars Single Del or Td Stars Double Del according to the screen type.

definitions.name: TD STARS GRAVITY
definitions.documentation: Set the direction which the stars shall drift into. 'sx' contains a value which is added each step to the horizontal speed and may be a positive number for right drift or a negative one for left movement.

definitions.name: TD STARS ACCELERATE ON
definitions.documentation: Determinates, if the stars are to be accelerated during the flight.

definitions.name: TD STARS ACCELERATE OFF
definitions.documentation: Determinates, if the stars are to be accelerated during the flight.

definitions.name: TD STARS PLANES
definitions.documentation: Td Stars Planes is used to specify the Bitplanes which the stars should be drawn on. Normally, these are the bitplanes 0 and 1, but these defaults can be modified with this instruction.

definitions.name: SDEEK
definitions.documentation: The Sdeek function reads a Word from the EVEN memory address 'address', exactly the AMOS function Deek. However, Bit 15 is used as sign bit so the result will be a value between -32678 and 32677.

definitions.name: SPEEK
definitions.documentation: The Speek function reads a Byte from the memory address 'address', exactly the AMOS function Peek. However, Bit 7 is used as sign bit so the result will be a value between -128 and 127.

definitions.name: PIX SHIFT UP
definitions.documentation: Using this command, you can increase the colour indexes in the rectangular area from x1,y1 to x2y2. The 'screen' parameter therefore contains the number of the screen on which this regions is situated.

signatures.presentation: Pix Shift Up screen, c1, c2, x1, y1 To x2, y2
Pix Shift Up screen, c1, c2, x1, y1 To x2, y2, bank

definitions.name: PIX SHIFT DOWN
definitions.documentation: Using this command, you can decrease the colour indexes in the rectangular area from x1,y1 to x2y2. The 'screen' parameter therefore contains the number of the screen on which this regions is situated.

signatures.presentation: Pix Shift Down screen, c1, c2, x1, y1 To x2, y2
Pix Shift Down screen, c1, c2, x1, y1 To x2, y2, bank

definitions.name: PIX BRIGHTEN
definitions.documentation: Using this command, you can increase the colour indexes in the rectangular area from x1,y1 to x2y2. The 'screen' parameter therefore contains the number of the screen on which this regions is situated.

signatures.presentation: Pix Brighten screen, c1, c2, x1, y1 To x2, y2
Pix Brighten screen, c1, c2, x1, y1 To x2, y2, bank

definitions.name: PIX DARKEN
definitions.documentation: Using this command, you can decrease the colour indexes in the rectangular area from x1,y1 to x2y2. The 'screen' parameter therefore contains the number of the screen on which this regions is situated.

signatures.presentation: Pix Darken screen, c1, c2, x1, y1 To x2, y2
Pix Darken screen, c1, c2, x1, y1 To x2, y2, bank

definitions.name: MAKE PIX MASK
definitions.documentation: Grabs a specific part of the screen and saves it into the bank with the number bank. This command can be used to create a mask for the Pix Shift instruction.

signatures.presentation: Make Pix Mask screen, x1, y1 To x2, y2, bank

definitions.name: COUNT PIXELS
definitions.documentation: Counts the pixels in the rectangular are from x1,y1 to x2,y2 in the screen with number screen, that don't have the colour index colour. This function can be used to first acquire the number of points in this area and then to reserve a coordinates bank.

signatures.presentation: Count Pixels(screen, colour, x1, y1 To x2, y2)

definitions.name: COORDS BANK
definitions.documentation: Reserves the bank numbered 'bank' to store a coordinates array. These banks are utilitised e.g for Splinters.

definitions.name: COORDS READ
definitions.documentation: This command is required to read the coordinates for Splinters into a bank. This memory bank with the number 'bank' must have been defined previously with a call to the Coords Bank command.

signatures.presentation: Coords Read screen, colour, x1, y1 To x2, y2, bank, mode

definitions.name: SPLINTERS BANK
definitions.documentation: Reserves a memory bank for a maximum of 'splinum' Splinters. Each Splinter requires 22 bytes of memory.

definitions.name: SPLINTERS LIMIT
definitions.documentation: Sets the limits for the Splinters to a rectangluar area on the screen. If you don't give any parameters, AMCAF uses the limits of the current screen.

signatures.presentation: Splinters Limit x1, y1 To x2, y2

definitions.name: SPLINTERS GRAVITY
definitions.documentation: Set the direction which the Splinters shall drift into. 'sx' contains a value which is added each step to the horizontal speed and may be a positive number for right drift or a negative one for left movement.

definitions.name: SPLINTERS INIT
definitions.documentation: Initialises the Splinters. They are fed with the coordinates and speeds you have specified earlier.

definitions.name: SPLINTERS COLOUR
definitions.documentation: Determinates, which colour is to be left, after a Splinter has released a dot from that point. The 'planes' parameter describes the number of bitplanes to be taken for the Splinters operation.

definitions.name: SPLINTERS SINGLE DO
definitions.documentation: Clears, draws and moves all Splinters in one single step. For Double Buffered screens you have to call the command Splinters Double Do, for normal ones Splinters Single Do.

definitions.name: SPLINTERS DOUBLE DO
definitions.documentation: Clears, draws and moves all Splinters in one single step. For Double Buffered screens you have to call the command Splinters Double Do, for normal ones Splinters Single Do.

definitions.name: SPLINTERS SINGLE DEL
definitions.documentation: Clears the splinters from the screen again. As the clearing process must either wipe the pre-last pixels from the screen (when using Double Buffering), or the last pixels (with Single Buffered screens), you have to take the appropriate command for the right screen type.

definitions.name: SPLINTERS DOUBLE DEL
definitions.documentation: Clears the splinters from the screen again. As the clearing process must either wipe the pre-last pixels from the screen (when using Double Buffering), or the last pixels (with Single Buffered screens), you have to take the appropriate command for the right screen type.

definitions.name: SPLINTERS MOVE
definitions.documentation: This command moves the Splinters one step.

definitions.name: SPLINTERS DRAW
definitions.documentation: Draws the Splinters onto the screen.

definitions.name: SPLINTERS MAX
definitions.documentation: Changes the max. amount of new Splinters to appear on each step.

definitions.name: SPLINTERS BACK
definitions.documentation: Saves the background, on which the Splinters are to be drawn in the next step.

definitions.name: IMPLODER UNPACK
definitions.documentation: Unpacks the File Imploder packed file, which is stored in the memory bank 'sbank', into the target bank 'tbank'. If the source bank does not contain a File Imploded file (which can be determinated be the first longword 'IMP!') a error message will be returned.

definitions.name: IMPLODER LOAD
definitions.documentation: This instruction tries to load and unpack a file-imploded file. The result will be stored in the bank numbered 'bank'.

definitions.name: LZSTR$
definitions.documentation: This function is nearly identical to Lsstr$ with the difference that leading zeros are not replaced by space characters. The parameter 'n' sets the number of digits the string should contain and can range from 1 to 10.

definitions.name: LSSTR$
definitions.documentation: Similar to the AMOS function Str$, Lsstr$ creates a string out of a number. However, with Lsstr$ the number will be created right justified with 'n' digits, leading zeros are replaced by spaces.

definitions.name: WRITE CLI
definitions.documentation: The Write Cli instruction writes the string 'text$' into the CLI window, AMOS or your program has been start from. If this window does not exist, no text will be output.

definitions.name: MIX COLOUR
definitions.documentation: The first version of the function mixes the two colours 'rgb1' and 'rgb2' and returns the resulting colour value.

signatures.presentation: Mix Colour(oldrgb, addrgb, lrgb To urgb)

definitions.name: CD DATE$
definitions.documentation: This simple function returns the date stamp 'date' as string in the format 'WWW DD-MMM-YY'.

definitions.name: CT TIME$
definitions.documentation: This very handy function converts the normal parameter 'time' into a string in the format 'HH:MM:SS'.

definitions.name: SPLINTERS FUEL
definitions.documentation: This command is used to set the amount of time the Splinters move over the screen until they disappear automatically. The 'time' parameter holds the number of steps the splinters are moved before they vanish.

definitions.name: SPLINTERS ACTIVE
definitions.documentation: This function returns how many Splinters are active at the moment. This can be used to know when the animation has finished.

definitions.name: SHADE BOB MASK
definitions.documentation: Using Shade Bob Mask you may specify, if the mask of a object or the first bitplane is to be taken for drawing. If flag is set to zero, bitplane 0 is used for the Shade Bob Up and Shade Bob Down instructions, other values for flag instruct the commands to use the mask of the object.

definitions.name: SHADE BOB PLANES
definitions.documentation: Set the amount of Bitplanes to be cycled through. This can be used to protect the graphics in higher bitplanes from the influences of Shade Bobs.

definitions.name: SHADE BOB UP
definitions.documentation: This instruction draws a Shade Bob on the screen numbered 'screen' at the coordinates x,y. This bob only increases the colour indexes only.

signatures.presentation: Shade Bob Up screen, x, y, image

definitions.name: SHADE BOB DOWN
definitions.documentation: This instruction draws a Shade Bob on the screen numbered 'screen' at the coordinates x,y. This bob only decreases the colour indexes only.

signatures.presentation: Shade Bob Down screen, x, y, image

definitions.name: HAM FADE OUT
definitions.documentation: As HAM screens cannot be faded out with the normal Fade commands, AMCAF provides you with a world sensation: The HAM fader! Although you can only fade out pictures, it's more than nothing, isn't it?

definitions.name: BANK DELTA ENCODE
definitions.documentation: Bank Delta Encode encodes a memory bank with the so-called delta algo. This is not packing, however, but yields better pack ratios on 8-bit sound samples.

signatures.presentation: Bank Delta Encode startaddress To endaddress

definitions.name: BANK DELTA DECODE
definitions.documentation: These commands decode a previously delta-encoded memory region. See Bank Delta Encode for more details on delta encoding.

signatures.presentation: Bank Delta Decode startaddress To endaddress

definitions.name: TURBO DRAW
definitions.documentation: This instruction replaces the AMOS Draw command. The x1,y1 coordinates represent the starting point of the line and x2,y2 the ending point.

signatures.presentation: Turbo Draw x1, y1 To x2, y2, c
Turbo Draw x1, y1 To x2, y2, c, bitplanes

definitions.name: BLITTER FILL
definitions.documentation: With Blitter Fill you can fill polygons. However, there are some limitations: the filling algorithm of the Blitter chip is very simple.

signatures.presentation: Blitter Fill sourcescreen, sourceplane To targetscreen, targetplane
Blitter Fill screen, bitplane, x1, y1, x2, y2
Blitter Fill ss, sp, x1, y1, x2, y2 To ts, tp

definitions.name: PT PLAY
definitions.documentation: Pt Play stars a Protracker music module which has be situated in memory bank number 'bank'. Optionally, you can specify a song position, which the music should be played from.

definitions.name: PT STOP
definitions.documentation: Stops the current music module. You can restart the music with the Pt Continue command from the very position you stopped it.

definitions.name: PT SIGNAL
definitions.documentation: The various music trackers Soundtracker, Noisetracker, Startrekker or Protracker feature effect commands e.g to change the volume of a note, or for portamento. Example for a Protracker noteline: setzen oder für Portamento.

definitions.name: PT VOLUME
definitions.documentation: The Pt Volume instruction sets the volume of the music. The parameter 'value' may range from 0 (silent) to 64 (full volume).

definitions.name: PT VOICE
definitions.documentation: This command defines the channels which should be used for the music and which should be free for e.g sound effects.

definitions.name: PT VU
definitions.documentation: Allows you to read the current volume of channel number 'channel'. If a new note is played, 'vol' contains the volume level else 0.

definitions.name: PT CIA SPEED
definitions.documentation: With the help of this command, you can set the speed for replaying the Protracker modules. However, the music may change the speed on it's own if it has been composed for CIA-Timing.

definitions.name: QSIN
definitions.documentation: This is a function to replace the original sine function of AMOS. It is faster and even allows you to multiple the value with the a parameter.

definitions.name: QCOS
definitions.documentation: This is a function to replace the original cosine function of AMOS. It is faster and even allows you to multiple the value with the a parameter.

definitions.name: VEC ROT POS
definitions.documentation: Using Vec Rot Pos you can change the position of the camera. 'posx' and 'posy' hold the movement position of the camera in X and Y direction.

signatures.presentation: Vec Rot Pos posx, posy, posz

definitions.name: VEC ROT ANGLES
definitions.documentation: Sets the three viewing angles, which are used for the vector rotation. 'angx' represents the angle, the coordinate is to be rotated along the X-axis.

signatures.presentation: Vec Rot Angles angx, angy, angz

definitions.name: VEC ROT PRECALC
definitions.documentation: This command must be called before every vector rotations, always after you have changed the viewing angle. It calculates a internal matrix that holds the results of often needed calculations which are required for the rotation in three dimensions.

definitions.name: VEC ROT X
definitions.documentation: This function calculates the X coordinate, which results in the vector rotation of a three dimensional point x,y,z around all three axis. This coordinate is automatically projected from 3D to 2D by dividing it through the distance.

definitions.name: VEC ROT Y
definitions.documentation: Calculates the new Y coordinate after the rotation. For more detailed information see Vec Rot X.

definitions.name: CHANGE PRINT FONT
definitions.documentation: Change Print Font changes the charset which is used for the Print command. This font always 8x8 pixels big and contains 256 characters, which is stored in a memory bank of exactly 2 KB (=2048 bytes).

definitions.name: QRND
definitions.documentation: Qrnd is totally identical to the Rnd function, with the only difference, that this one is much faster.

definitions.name: VEC ROT Z
definitions.documentation: Calculates the new Z coordinate after the rotation. Please note that the current camera position has already been added to this value.

definitions.name: COP POS
definitions.documentation: If you create your own copperlist, you can use this function to remember the position of the next copper instruction. Later you can then write to this address directly to change the values of a copper instruction.

definitions.name: MAKE BANK FONT
definitions.documentation: Using this powerful command you can store any amiga font in a memory bank. The current font on the current screen will be taken to create the font bank numbered 'bank'.

definitions.name: CHANGE BANK FONT
definitions.documentation: This is a really sensible command which sets the text font on the current screen to the one saved in the memory bank numbered 'bank'.

definitions.name: BLITTER CLEAR
definitions.documentation: The Blitter chip can be used to clear Bitplanes as well. In comparison to the AMOS command Cls, Blitter Clear allows you to wipe single bitplane instead of all.

signatures.presentation: Blitter Clear screen, bitplane, x1, y1 To x2, y2

definitions.name: BLITTER BUSY
definitions.documentation: This function returns -1 (True), if the Blitter chip is currently busy and working on a job, e.g is currently clearing a bitplane or drawing a line etc. This can be used to wait for the end of the blitter activity, although Blitter Wait is preferred in this case.

definitions.name: BLITTER WAIT
definitions.documentation: This command waits until the Blitter has finished his work. You are adviced to use this command before calling e.g Print or any other command that does not require the blitter, that will draw onto a screen, which is currently altered by the blitter.

definitions.name: SHADE PIX
definitions.documentation: This instructions increases the colour value at the given point x,y on the current screen. If the highest colour is reached, the colour is resetted to zero.

definitions.name: BLITTER COPY LIMIT
definitions.documentation: Using this command you define the rectangluar area which will be used for the Blitter Copy command. If you only specify the 'screen' parameter, the full screen dimensions of the screen numbered 'screen' will be taken.

signatures.presentation: Blitter Copy Limit x1, y1 To x2, y2

definitions.name: BLITTER COPY
definitions.documentation: With the help of Blitter Copy you can copy a Bitplane of a screen to another or the same bitplane on another or the same screen. The Amiga Blitter chip is used to fulfill this action.

signatures.presentation: Blitter Copy sourcescreen, sourceplane To targetscreen, targetplane
Blitter Copy sourcescreen, sourceplane To targetscreen, targetplane, miniterm
Blitter Copy s1, p1, s2, p2 To targetscreen, targetplane
Blitter Copy s1, p1, s2, p2 To targetscreen, targetplane, miniterm
Blitter Copy s1, p1, s2, p2, s3, p3 To targetscreen, targetplane
Blitter Copy s1, p1, s2, p2, s3, p3 To targetscreen, targetplane, miniterm

definitions.name: SET RAIN COLOUR
definitions.documentation: With the help of this command you can change the colour index of a rainbow which has been set previously by Set Rainbow to a new value. This means that you can remove the irretating limit to the first 16 colours and are now able to access all 32 colours.

definitions.name: RAIN FADE
definitions.documentation: Using this powerful instruction you can fade the rainbow numbered 'rainbownr' out or to other colours. With the first version of Rain Fade you may specify the target colour '$RGB' to which all colours of the rainbow will be faded.

definitions.name: QSQR
definitions.documentation: Calculates the square root from the value 'value'. However, it only works with integer and is faster than the AMOS square root function =Sqr.

definitions.name: BCIRCLE
definitions.documentation: Draws an empty circle around x,y with radius 'r' into the bitplane 'plane'. This line is really only one pixel thick to ensure the circle can be filled by the Blitter chip.

definitions.name: PT DATA BASE
definitions.documentation: Returns the starting address of the internal datatable of the protracker replay routine. Please don't write into this area randomly.

definitions.name: PT INSTR ADDRESS
definitions.documentation: Returns the starting address of the music instrument with the number 'instnr' of the current Protracker module.

definitions.name: PT INSTR LENGTH
definitions.documentation: Returns the length of the instrument 'instnr'.

definitions.name: PT BANK
definitions.documentation: This command is used if you want to play back instruments from a music module but the music bank has not yet been specified with Pt Play.

definitions.name: PT INSTR PLAY
definitions.documentation: The Pt Instr Play instruction replays an instrument of the current Protracker music module.

signatures.presentation: Pt Instr Play voice, instnr, freq

definitions.name: PT SAM STOP
definitions.documentation: Stops a sound effect on the channels, which are defined in the bitmap 'voice'. This is valid for samples started by Pt Sam Play, Pt Instr Play and Pt Raw Play.

definitions.name: PT RAW PLAY
definitions.documentation: This command corresponds to the normal AMOS command Sam Raw. Pt Raw Play replays a piece of memory as audio sample.

signatures.presentation: Pt Raw Play voice, address, length, freq

definitions.name: PT SAM BANK
definitions.documentation: If you want to replay standard AMOS samples with AMCAF, you have to inform AMCAF about the Sample-Bank, which contains the sound effects. And that's exactly what Pt Sam Bank does.

definitions.name: PT SAM PLAY
definitions.documentation: The Pt Sam Play command plays a sample out of an AMOS sample bank. The advantage to the normal Sam Play instruction is that the sounds 'interact' with the Protracker music.

signatures.presentation: Pt Sam Play voice, samnr, freq

definitions.name: PT SAM VOLUME
definitions.documentation: This instruction sets the volume for the following sample replays. The 'volume' paramter must range from 0 to 64.

definitions.name: PAL GET SCREEN
definitions.documentation: This functions stores the complete palette of screen number 'screen' in the interior palette memory number 'palnr'. 'palnr' must be a value between 0 and 7.

definitions.name: PAL SET SCREEN
definitions.documentation: Writes back the previously stored colour palette 'palnr' to the screen number 'screen'.

definitions.name: PAL GET
definitions.documentation: This function reads the value of the colour with the number index 'index' from the internal stored palette buffer 'palnr'.

definitions.name: PAL SET
definitions.documentation: Changes the colour entry numbered 'index' in the palette with the number 'palnr' to the colour value 'colour'. palnr must be range from 0 to 7.

definitions.name: EXCHANGE BOB
definitions.documentation: This command simply swaps the two images i1 and i2 in the current sprite bank. i1 and i2 must exist as a valid image, otherwise an error will be reported.

definitions.name: EXCHANGE ICON
definitions.documentation: This command simply swaps the two images i1 and i2 in the current icon bank. i1 and i2 must exist as a valid image, otherwise an error will be reported.

definitions.name: BEST PEN
definitions.documentation: This function acquires the pen which is nearest to the colour $RGB. Optionally, you can give the range to search for the best colour.

definitions.name: BZOOM
definitions.documentation: Using this command you can zoom a region on the screen to a integer multiple very fast. As result the graphics are double, four times or eight times as wide and from 1 to 15 times as high as before.

signatures.presentation: Bzoom s1, x1, y1, x2, y2 To s2, x3, y3, factor

definitions.name: X SMOUSE
definitions.documentation: Returns the x coordinate of the second mouse.

definitions.name: Y SMOUSE
definitions.documentation: Returns the y coordinate of the second mouse.

definitions.name: SMOUSE X
definitions.documentation: Sets the x coordinate of the mouse to xpos.

definitions.name: SMOUSE Y
definitions.documentation: Sets the y coordinate of the mouse to ypos.

definitions.name: SMOUSE SPEED
definitions.documentation: Sets the speed of the mouse. value therefore represents the factor by which power of 2 the mouse should be slowed down.

definitions.name: SMOUSE KEY
definitions.documentation: Returns the state of the mouse buttons in port 1.

definitions.name: LIMIT SMOUSE
definitions.documentation: Defines the region in which the mouse can be moved on the screen. If the parameters are omitted, the full size of the current screen will be used as default.

signatures.presentation: Limit Smouse x1, y1 To x2, y2

definitions.name: XFIRE
definitions.documentation: This function returns the state of a possibly existant second fire button on a joystick or joypad in the gameport numbered port. If the lowlevel- library is available, all the other buttons can be checked aswell.

definitions.name: PT CONTINUE
definitions.documentation: Restarts the protracker music at that point it has previously been stopped using Pt Stop. Do not attempt use Pt Continue without having started the music once with Pt Play.

definitions.name: PT CPATTERN
definitions.documentation: This function returns the current pattern/song position. This can be used to restart a song at a given position when you stopped the music with Pt Stop.

definitions.name: PT CPOS
definitions.documentation: This function returns the current 'row' of the songs current pattern. The returned value is a number between 0 and 63.

definitions.name: PT CINSTR
definitions.documentation: Pt Cinstr returns the instrument being played on music channel chan. num is therefore a value between 0 and 31, whereas 0 tells you, that no sample has been trigged at that very moment.

definitions.name: PT CNOTE
definitions.documentation: Pt Cnote returns the frequency of an instrument being played on music channel chan at that very moment.

definitions.name: PT SAM FREQ
definitions.documentation: This command changes the frequency of the sample currently being played on channel chan to the new frequency in freq. If no sample is currently played on this channel, it will have no effect nor will it have effect on the protracker music.

definitions.name: VCLIP
definitions.documentation: The Vclip function works like a Min(Max(val,lower),upper) expression, it restricts a value val into its lower and upper boundaries.

definitions.name: VIN
definitions.documentation: The Vin function will create an exquisite bottle of wine... Hehe ;-)

definitions.name: VMOD
definitions.documentation: Vmod restricts a value val into the lower and upper boundaries. However, it does it in another way compared to Vclip.

definitions.name: INSSTR$
definitions.documentation: Inserts the string b$ at the position pos into string a$.

definitions.name: CUTSTR$
definitions.documentation: Cuts out the part of the string s$ from letter position pos1 to pos2.

definitions.name: REPLACESTR$
definitions.documentation: This function searches in the string s$ for occurences of search$ and replaces them with the string replace$.

definitions.name: ITEMSTR$
definitions.documentation: This is a very handy function that helps you to avoid little string arrays where actually no are required. s$ contains a number of so called items, which are numbered increasingly from zero onwards.

definitions.name: QARC
definitions.documentation: This function will return the angle to a point at the relative coordinates deltax,deltay. angle is in the same format used with Qsin and Qcos, so an angle of 360 degree is equalent to 1024.

definitions.name: EVEN
definitions.documentation: Returns True (-1), if a number is even or False (0), if a number is odd.

definitions.name: ODD
definitions.documentation: Returns True (-1), if a number is odd or False (0), if a number is even.

definitions.name: HAM POINT
definitions.documentation: This function simplifies the process of finding the right colour of a point at the coordinates x,y on a HAM screen. There's no need to proceed from the left to the right until the right pixel value is found, although it the Ham Colour method is a little bit faster when processing a picture from left to the right, whereas Ham Point can access any point on the screen indiviually without preprocessing.

definitions.name: SET OBJECT DATE
definitions.documentation: This command changes the date of the Object 'pathfile$' to the given date and time stamp.

signatures.presentation: Set Object Date pathfile$, date, time

definitions.name: AGA DETECT
definitions.documentation: This is a simple function that checks for the existance of the AGA chipset, found in A1200, A4000 and CD³² machines. It returns -1 (True), if the new chipset is mounted or 0 (False) if the ecs or ocs chipsets are still in use.

definitions.name: PAL SPREAD
definitions.documentation: Creates a smooth blend between the two colours rgb1 and rgb2. The resulting colour set will be stored between c1 and c2.

signatures.presentation: Pal Spread c1, rgb1 To c2, rgb2

definitions.name: CT STRING
definitions.documentation: This function will evaluate the valid time in the time$ string and then return the time as number or -1, if the string has been invalid.

definitions.name: CD STRING
definitions.documentation: This function will evaluate the valid date in the date$ string and then return the date stamp or -1, if the string has been invalid.

definitions.name: C2P CONVERT
definitions.documentation: Converts a chunky pixel buffer to planar format and blits it to a screen at the given offset.

signatures.presentation: C2p Convert st, wx, wy To screen, ox, oy

definitions.name: C2P SHIFT
definitions.documentation: Converts a chunky pixel buffer to planar format with a pixel shift and writes it to a second buffer.

signatures.presentation: C2p Shift st, wx, wy To st2, shift

definitions.name: C2P FIRE
definitions.documentation: Converts a chunky pixel buffer to planar format applying a subtraction/fire effect into a second buffer.

signatures.presentation: C2p Fire st, wx, wy To st2, sub

definitions.name: SLOAD
definitions.documentation: Loads a sample directly from disk into a sample bank slot, similar to the Music extension Sload.

definitions.name: SSAVE
definitions.documentation: Saves a sample bank slot to disk, similar to the Music extension Ssave.

definitions.name: PT FREE VOICE
definitions.documentation: Searches for the best free ProTracker channel to use for sample replay, based on a bitmask of candidate channels.

definitions.name: ALLOC TRANS SOURCE
definitions.documentation: Allocates the source bitplane data buffer used by the screen transition system.

definitions.name: SET TRANS SOURCE
definitions.documentation: Sets the source bank number or raw address for the screen transition system.

definitions.name: ALLOC TRANS MAP
definitions.documentation: Allocates the transition map buffer for a screen transition of given width and height.

signatures.presentation: Alloc Trans Map bank, width, height

definitions.name: SET TRANS MAP
definitions.documentation: Sets the transition map bank number or raw address, along with the map width and height.

signatures.presentation: Set Trans Map bank, width, height

definitions.name: ALLOC CODE BANK
definitions.documentation: Allocates a code bank of the specified size for use with the screen transition engine.

definitions.name: TRANS SCREEN RUNTIME
definitions.documentation: Performs a runtime (VBL-driven) screen transition on the specified screen and bitplane.

signatures.presentation: Trans Screen Runtime scr, bitplane, ox, oy

definitions.name: TRANS SCREEN STATIC
definitions.documentation: Performs a static (immediate) screen transition; not yet implemented in V1.50.

signatures.presentation: Trans Screen Static scr, bitplane, ox, oy

definitions.name: TRANS SCREEN DYNAMIC
definitions.documentation: Performs a dynamic screen transition on the specified screen and bitplane.

signatures.presentation: Trans Screen Dynamic scr, bitplane, ox, oy

definitions.name: TURBO TEXT
definitions.documentation: Prints text at screen coordinates using a bank font for high-speed rendering.

signatures.presentation: Turbo Text screen, x, y, text$
Turbo Text screen, x, y, text$, colour
